result.markdown:
--- title: "API" jupyter: python3 execute: enabled: true freeze: auto --- The public API is intentionally small. It describes one local PU/DO insertion problem, generates valid event orders, and lets your application test later feasibility layers. ## Core Concepts ```{mermaid} flowchart TD A["pickup
i"] --> B["drop-off
i + n"] C["open
drop-off"] --> D["may appear
anywhere"] ``` | Concept | Meaning | | --- | --- | | `requests` | New pickup labels. Labels must be unique and hashable. | | `dropoff_for` | Function mapping each pickup label to the paired drop-off label. | | `open_dropoffs` | Drop-off labels for requests already open before this local plan starts. | | `strategy` | Generation method: `"dfs"`, `"insertion"`, or `"topological"`. | When `dropoff_for` is omitted, integer pickup `i` maps to drop-off `i + n`, where `n` is the number of new requests. ## Generation ### `pudo_sequences` Return valid pickup/drop-off event sequences as a list. ```python from pudo_sequences import pudo_sequences routes = pudo_sequences([0, 1]) assert routes == [ (0, 1, 2, 3), (0, 1, 3, 2), (0, 2, 1, 3), (1, 0, 2, 3), (1, 0, 3, 2), (1, 3, 0, 2), ] ``` Use this when the complete local action set is small enough to materialize. ### `iter_pudo_sequences` Yield valid pickup/drop-off event sequences lazily. ```python from pudo_sequences import iter_pudo_sequences for order in iter_pudo_sequences([0, 1, 2]): # Replace this with capacity, time-window, load, cost, or reward logic. print(order) ``` Shared parameters: | Parameter | Meaning | | --- | --- | | `requests` | Iterable of new pickup labels. | | `open_dropoffs` | Iterable of unconstrained drop-off labels already open at planning time. | | `dropoff_for` | Callable from pickup label to drop-off label. Required for non-integer label schemes. | | `strategy` | `"dfs"` by default. `"insertion"` is dependency-free. `"topological"` requires NetworkX. | All event labels must be unique and hashable. Generated drop-off labels must not collide with pickup labels or open drop-offs. ## Open Drop-Offs and Custom Labels Already-open drop-offs represent requests whose pickups happened before the current local sequence. ```python from pudo_sequences import pudo_sequences routes = pudo_sequences([0, 1], open_dropoffs=[4]) assert (4, 0, 1, 2, 3) in routes assert (0, 1, 2, 3, 4) in routes ``` Use `dropoff_for` for non-default labels. ```python from pudo_sequences import pudo_sequences routes = pudo_sequences( [1, 2], dropoff_for=lambda pickup: f"{pickup}'", ) assert (1, "1'", 2, "2'") in routes ``` ## Counts ### `count_pudo_sequences` Count valid labeled pickup/drop-off sequences. ```python from pudo_sequences import count_pudo_sequences assert count_pudo_sequences(3) == 90 assert count_pudo_sequences(3, n_open_dropoffs=2) == 5040 ``` For `n` new requests and `m` already-open drop-offs: ```text (2n + m)! / 2^n ``` ### `count_fixed_pickup_order_dropoff_insertions` Count drop-off insertion patterns after fixing one pickup order. ```python from pudo_sequences import count_fixed_pickup_order_dropoff_insertions assert count_fixed_pickup_order_dropoff_insertions(3) == 15 ``` This is useful for the constructive view: ```text choose pickup order -> insert each paired drop-off after its pickup ``` ### `count_open_dropoff_insertions` Count ways to insert labeled already-open drop-offs into an existing PU/DO sequence. ```python from pudo_sequences import count_open_dropoff_insertions assert count_open_dropoff_insertions(3, 2) == 56 ``` ## Prefix Index ### `build_pudo_index` Build a prefix index over all complete PU/DO sequences for a local problem. ```python from pudo_sequences import build_pudo_index index = build_pudo_index([0, 1]) assert index.continuations([0, 1]) == {(2, 3), (3, 2)} assert index.continuations([0, 2]) == {(1, 3)} ``` Use this when a search, policy, or repeated classroom example asks which suffixes remain possible after a committed prefix. ### `PudoSequenceIndex` `PudoSequenceIndex` can also be built from precomputed sequences. ```python from pudo_sequences import PudoSequenceIndex index = PudoSequenceIndex.from_sequences([(1, 2), (1, 3)]) assert index.sequences() == {(1, 2), (1, 3)} assert index.continuations([1]) == {(2,), (3,)} ``` ## Generation Strategies ```{mermaid} flowchart TD A["same valid
sequence set"] --> B["DFS
state expansion"] A --> C["pickup order
and insertion"] A --> D["topological
sorts"] A --> E["prefix trie
for reuse"] ``` All generation strategies represent the same constrained sequence set. | Strategy | What it teaches | | --- | --- | | `"dfs"` | Expand the state space by choosing available pickups and available drop-offs. | | `"insertion"` | Choose a pickup order, then insert each drop-off after its pickup. | | `"topological"` | Treat each `pickup_i -> dropoff_i` relation as an edge in a precedence graph. | | `build_pudo_index` | Precompute the sequence set as a prefix trie for repeated continuation lookup. | The table below is generated during the maintainer docs build. It gives a small scaling profile for `n = 1..5`; exact timings depend on the machine. ::: {#a214ad45 .cell message='false' execution_count=1} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ```{=html}
RequestsValid sequencesDFS msInsertion msTopological msIndex build msContinuation lookup ms
110.090.040.210.060.02
260.060.070.460.080.03
3900.380.254.131.810.27
425209.645.85129.1918.839.03
5113400493.35228.095461.341243.45412.81
``` ::: ::: Full enumeration grows factorially. Use these functions for small local neighborhoods, exact validation, teaching, and candidate insertion sets inside a larger dispatch, optimization, or learning system.
